869.spatial_relations_text:
--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Stability Prediction) ---
- Objects placed flat with their center of gravity within the effective base of support are stable by default.
- Do not predict dramatic or chain-reaction collapses in everyday scenes unless there is clearly insufficient physical support, a steep incline, or a significant external force.
- Perturbations where the agent's mass is less than 1% of the structure's mass (e.g., a bird landing, a raindrop) have no significant effect.
- Perturbations where the agent's mass is greater than 10% of the structure's mass (e.g., a person climbing a light ladder) cause significant displacement or tipping.
- Forces applied above an object's center of gravity tend to cause tipping, while forces applied below tend to cause sliding.
- Precision-balanced structures (e.g., card houses, objects balanced on a point) are extremely vulnerable to minor vibrations, whereas anchored or heavy structures (e.g., lamp posts, trees, wall-mounted TVs) are unaffected by everyday light impacts.
- When determining the 'least stable' object, assess only the geometric ratio of the base of support relative to the height. Fragility does not equal instability.
- On inclines, objects with curved contact surfaces (spheres, sideways cylinders) will roll at minor tilts of 1-2 degrees. Objects with flat bases rely on friction and typically remain stationary up to 5-15 degrees.
- When an object is removed from an arrangement, only the objects that directly depend on it for support will fall. Objects with independent support remain unaffected.
- When a person sits or jumps on heavy furniture (e.g., beds, sofas, benches), the furniture itself remains stable. Only loose, lightweight items on the surface (e.g., pillows, loose papers, unanchored blankets) will bounce or be displaced.
- Humans possess active muscle reflexes to recover balance. They will not fall uncontrollably from minor obstacles or slight forces unless the environment is extremely steep or slippery.

- A flexible object wrapped around the outside of another object (e.g., a chain around a gear) inherently has a larger effective physical radius than the enclosed object.
- When evaluating if container A can hold object B, you must subtract the volume of any items already inside container A. Do not rely solely on exterior dimensions.
- Interactions must respect structural mechanics. A tree cannot support the weight of a concrete building; a human body is too short to bridge a wide canal; a tall vertical structure (like a lighthouse) lacks the structural design to act as a horizontal bridge.
- Biological structures have natural growth limits (e.g., inner flower parts will not grow endlessly beyond outer petals). Manufactured items like shoes scale in 3D; a larger size means increased width and volume, not just length.
- Spherical transparent containers globally magnify the objects inside them. Cylindrical transparent containers only stretch objects horizontally.
- Large printed graphics on a surface (e.g., a giant book printed on a tote bag) create a visual illusion of size but do not increase the object's actual physical volume or capacity.
- Due to massive scale disparities, a small foreground object (like a potted plant) can only occlude a tiny fraction (like a single branch) of a massive background object (like a distant tree). It cannot cover half or most of it.
- Using a shorter background set or backdrop reveals more of the actual room behind it, making the entire space appear more expanded and spacious.

--- Domain Rules (Scale Consistency) ---
- Capacity depends on 3D internal volume (depth, width, opening), not just 2D footprint or length. Without 3D data, differently shaped containers are non-measurable. Sugarcane yield depends on thickness, not just length.
- Appropriateness requires matching functional capacity to the space. A single small AC or a small chandelier is insufficient for a large room/hall, regardless of whether it physically 'fits'.
- When evaluating 'fit inside', consider all valid placements: internal shelves, top ledges, or vertical stacking (e.g., standardized laundry appliances are stackable).
- A background or backdrop makes the foreground object appear BIGGER and more prominent through framing contrast, not smaller.
- Objects appearing smaller in the distance (e.g., top stairs, distant fruits) is purely perspective shrinkage. Physically, they are the same size as the closer ones.
- Mass-produced industrial items (e.g., thread spools, bowls, water dispensers) are physically identical in size. Apparent size differences in a single image are always due to perspective or angle.
- Biological reality overrides naive visual impressions: a baby is small enough to ride a dog; a bird's neck can extend significantly; a tree's leaf canopy visually inflates its mass.
- Seating capacity relies on table perimeter (approx. 60cm per person). Two long tables can easily seat 10+ people. Do not default to small fixed numbers without visual proof.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Gravity Effects) ---
- Dense, compact objects fall at similar speeds regardless of their specific shape; their exact impact times rely on specific environmental factors. Only lightweight, thin objects with high surface-area-to-mass ratios fall significantly slower due to air resistance.
- Poured liquids always follow a curved (parabolic) downward trajectory and spread outward immediately upon impacting a surface.
- Rolling strictly requires a curved contact patch with the surface. Objects with flat bases, irregular contact areas, or symmetry-breaking protrusions will not roll, regardless of their overall shape. Round objects roll continuously on slopes, but require an initial kinetic push to roll temporarily on flat surfaces.
- Gravity alone does not break the equilibrium of a stable resting object; movement requires a specific external trigger (e.g., wind, bump, vibration).
- When a supporting surface is tilted slowly and gradually, static friction keeps objects completely stationary until a specific angle threshold is exceeded.
- Rapidly removing a bottom supporting object causes upper objects to drop vertically in place due to inertia. Slowly removing it causes the upper objects to slide or topple as support is gradually lost.
- Buoyancy depends on overall average density. Objects with trapped internal air pockets will float, but physically altering the object to expose or remove those air pockets (e.g., by slicing or crushing) will cause it to sink.
- Collisions involving either a rigid object hitting a soft/elastic surface, or an elastic object hitting a hard surface, will result in a bounce rather than a dead stop.
- Wind-blown lightweight flexible materials (like fabric) do not float perfectly straight; they follow air currents and will wrap around or cling to physical obstacles in their path.
- An obstacle's ability to stop a moving object depends entirely on their relative mass and speed. Light or small obstacles will not stop heavy or fast-moving objects and will simply be knocked aside.
- Objects cannot balance or stand in orientations where their geometry and center of mass fail to form a stable equilibrium.
- If the camera angle obscures the spatial relationship, trajectory path, or landing zone of a falling or moving object, the physical outcome must be evaluated as 'cannot be determined'.
- Always assume the most direct, intuitive, and simple physical outcome for an action (e.g., gently pushed items slide, liquids spread) without over-analyzing for dramatic or extreme results.

--- Domain Rules (Tool Handedness) ---
- The Handle Orientation Rule: If the handle of a tool (such as a spoon, pan, or mop) is pointing to or positioned on the LEFT side of the user's perspective or a reference object, it is ergonomically optimized to be picked up with the LEFT hand. If positioned on the RIGHT, it favors the RIGHT hand.
- The Precision vs. Gross Motor Rule: A person's handedness (dominant hand) is most evident in actions requiring fine motor control and precision (e.g., scooping spice, writing, stirring, manipulating small objects). It is LEAST evident in gross motor actions, passive holding, or bilateral tasks (e.g., plugging a charger, placing a frame, simply holding a phone, or typing).
- The Active Execution Rule: When interacting with an object like a kettle or a tool, the active execution movement (e.g., lifting, pouring, or scooping) is the primary indicator of handedness, rather than passive stabilization actions (e.g., merely holding the handle or placing a lid).

888.spatial_relations_text:
Bypassed via Pure VLM.
--- Domain Rules (Accessibility Constraints) ---
- Systematically count every distinct physical item blocking an access point. For grouped items or stacks, count each visually distinguishable unit separately. Do not stop at only the most salient objects.
- An access zone includes the entire physical footprint needed to approach, operate, and pass through an opening (e.g., a door's swing arc). Any object intersecting this extended zone is a blocker.
- A 'direct path' requires continuous, same-surface traversal. Solid physical dividers (e.g., medians, barriers, grade separations) block direct paths.
- To identify a specific obstructing object, trace a direct approach line to the target. The first object intersecting this line (including items protruding from above or below) is the primary obstructor.
- Human vertical reach is limited (~2.0-2.3m). Objects or surfaces above ground-level reach (e.g., high shelves, ceilings) are inaccessible without visual evidence of specialized equipment.
- Select the most specific, visually verifiable reason for inaccessibility (e.g., 'no visible stairs'). Avoid defaulting to compound answers like 'all of the above' unless every factor is independently confirmed.
- 'Available' strictly means physically present, unoccupied, and usable. Occupied slots or seats count as zero availability.
- Evaluate obstructions for different directions or zones independently. Multiple areas can be obstructed simultaneously, so always verify compound options (e.g., 'both X and Y').
- In structured environments (e.g., aisles, marked lots), assume a path is accessible if no physical obstacles are visibly present. The absence of visible blockage means the path is clear.
- Limit a blocked zone strictly to the physical footprint of the obstructing object. Do not extrapolate a blockage to adjacent, unoccluded access points.

894.spatial_relations_text:
--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Stability Prediction) ---
- Objects placed flat with their center of gravity within the effective base of support are stable by default.
- Do not predict dramatic or chain-reaction collapses in everyday scenes unless there is clearly insufficient physical support, a steep incline, or a significant external force.
- Perturbations where the agent's mass is less than 1% of the structure's mass (e.g., a bird landing, a raindrop) have no significant effect.
- Perturbations where the agent's mass is greater than 10% of the structure's mass (e.g., a person climbing a light ladder) cause significant displacement or tipping.
- Forces applied above an object's center of gravity tend to cause tipping, while forces applied below tend to cause sliding.
- Precision-balanced structures (e.g., card houses, objects balanced on a point) are extremely vulnerable to minor vibrations, whereas anchored or heavy structures (e.g., lamp posts, trees, wall-mounted TVs) are unaffected by everyday light impacts.
- When determining the 'least stable' object, assess only the geometric ratio of the base of support relative to the height. Fragility does not equal instability.
- On inclines, objects with curved contact surfaces (spheres, sideways cylinders) will roll at minor tilts of 1-2 degrees. Objects with flat bases rely on friction and typically remain stationary up to 5-15 degrees.
- When an object is removed from an arrangement, only the objects that directly depend on it for support will fall. Objects with independent support remain unaffected.
- When a person sits or jumps on heavy furniture (e.g., beds, sofas, benches), the furniture itself remains stable. Only loose, lightweight items on the surface (e.g., pillows, loose papers, unanchored blankets) will bounce or be displaced.
- Humans possess active muscle reflexes to recover balance. They will not fall uncontrollably from minor obstacles or slight forces unless the environment is extremely steep or slippery.

897.spatial_relations_text:
Bypassed via Pure VLM.
--- Domain Rules (Scale Consistency) ---
- Capacity depends on 3D internal volume (depth, width, opening), not just 2D footprint or length. Without 3D data, differently shaped containers are non-measurable. Sugarcane yield depends on thickness, not just length.
- Appropriateness requires matching functional capacity to the space. A single small AC or a small chandelier is insufficient for a large room/hall, regardless of whether it physically 'fits'.
- When evaluating 'fit inside', consider all valid placements: internal shelves, top ledges, or vertical stacking (e.g., standardized laundry appliances are stackable).
- A background or backdrop makes the foreground object appear BIGGER and more prominent through framing contrast, not smaller.
- Objects appearing smaller in the distance (e.g., top stairs, distant fruits) is purely perspective shrinkage. Physically, they are the same size as the closer ones.
- Mass-produced industrial items (e.g., thread spools, bowls, water dispensers) are physically identical in size. Apparent size differences in a single image are always due to perspective or angle.
- Biological reality overrides naive visual impressions: a baby is small enough to ride a dog; a bird's neck can extend significantly; a tree's leaf canopy visually inflates its mass.
- Seating capacity relies on table perimeter (approx. 60cm per person). Two long tables can easily seat 10+ people. Do not default to small fixed numbers without visual proof.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Gravity Effects) ---
- Dense, compact objects fall at similar speeds regardless of their specific shape; their exact impact times rely on specific environmental factors. Only lightweight, thin objects with high surface-area-to-mass ratios fall significantly slower due to air resistance.
- Poured liquids always follow a curved (parabolic) downward trajectory and spread outward immediately upon impacting a surface.
- Rolling strictly requires a curved contact patch with the surface. Objects with flat bases, irregular contact areas, or symmetry-breaking protrusions will not roll, regardless of their overall shape. Round objects roll continuously on slopes, but require an initial kinetic push to roll temporarily on flat surfaces.
- Gravity alone does not break the equilibrium of a stable resting object; movement requires a specific external trigger (e.g., wind, bump, vibration).
- When a supporting surface is tilted slowly and gradually, static friction keeps objects completely stationary until a specific angle threshold is exceeded.
- Rapidly removing a bottom supporting object causes upper objects to drop vertically in place due to inertia. Slowly removing it causes the upper objects to slide or topple as support is gradually lost.
- Buoyancy depends on overall average density. Objects with trapped internal air pockets will float, but physically altering the object to expose or remove those air pockets (e.g., by slicing or crushing) will cause it to sink.
- Collisions involving either a rigid object hitting a soft/elastic surface, or an elastic object hitting a hard surface, will result in a bounce rather than a dead stop.
- Wind-blown lightweight flexible materials (like fabric) do not float perfectly straight; they follow air currents and will wrap around or cling to physical obstacles in their path.
- An obstacle's ability to stop a moving object depends entirely on their relative mass and speed. Light or small obstacles will not stop heavy or fast-moving objects and will simply be knocked aside.
- Objects cannot balance or stand in orientations where their geometry and center of mass fail to form a stable equilibrium.
- If the camera angle obscures the spatial relationship, trajectory path, or landing zone of a falling or moving object, the physical outcome must be evaluated as 'cannot be determined'.
- Always assume the most direct, intuitive, and simple physical outcome for an action (e.g., gently pushed items slide, liquids spread) without over-analyzing for dramatic or extreme results.

905.spatial_relations_text:
--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Stability Prediction) ---
- Objects placed flat with their center of gravity within the effective base of support are stable by default.
- Do not predict dramatic or chain-reaction collapses in everyday scenes unless there is clearly insufficient physical support, a steep incline, or a significant external force.
- Perturbations where the agent's mass is less than 1% of the structure's mass (e.g., a bird landing, a raindrop) have no significant effect.
- Perturbations where the agent's mass is greater than 10% of the structure's mass (e.g., a person climbing a light ladder) cause significant displacement or tipping.
- Forces applied above an object's center of gravity tend to cause tipping, while forces applied below tend to cause sliding.
- Precision-balanced structures (e.g., card houses, objects balanced on a point) are extremely vulnerable to minor vibrations, whereas anchored or heavy structures (e.g., lamp posts, trees, wall-mounted TVs) are unaffected by everyday light impacts.
- When determining the 'least stable' object, assess only the geometric ratio of the base of support relative to the height. Fragility does not equal instability.
- On inclines, objects with curved contact surfaces (spheres, sideways cylinders) will roll at minor tilts of 1-2 degrees. Objects with flat bases rely on friction and typically remain stationary up to 5-15 degrees.
- When an object is removed from an arrangement, only the objects that directly depend on it for support will fall. Objects with independent support remain unaffected.
- When a person sits or jumps on heavy furniture (e.g., beds, sofas, benches), the furniture itself remains stable. Only loose, lightweight items on the surface (e.g., pillows, loose papers, unanchored blankets) will bounce or be displaced.
- Humans possess active muscle reflexes to recover balance. They will not fall uncontrollably from minor obstacles or slight forces unless the environment is extremely steep or slippery.

--- Domain Rules (Proximity Gradients) ---
- Positions described relative to the 'frame' or 'image boundaries', such as the center of the frame or the top of the image, refer exclusively to 2D Cartesian coordinates (X/Y axes) and must not be evaluated using 3D depth relative to the camera (Z axis).
- When two objects differ simultaneously in their horizontal positioning and their depth relative to the camera, their spatial alignment relative to each other is strictly diagonal, regardless of whether their front planes are oriented parallel to the camera.
- When assessing the closest or farthest depth of specific anatomical parts of a subject viewed in a side or diagonal profile, protruding extremities such as the nose, beak, or leading shoulder represent the extreme depth limits and supersede broader anatomical regions like the entire head or body.
- In evaluating the greatest proximity to a specific subject, direct physical contact or visual overlap by foreground environmental elements, such as surrounding grass, leaves, or branches, often takes precedence over biologically attached body parts that are positioned further back in the 3D space.
- The Euclidean distance between two objects within the scene cannot be accurately deduced merely by comparing their respective distances to the camera; objects sharing a direct structural baseline, such as being placed on the same table or identical shelving unit, possess greater proximity to each other than to isolated objects sharing a similar camera depth.

--- Domain Rules (Facing Direction) ---
- The Elevation/Attention Rule: When individuals are described as looking at an elevated environmental feature, such as a fountain flowing from the top of rocks, or standing on a far bank looking across a deep space, their facial direction should strictly be classified as 'Upward' due to the upward gaze required.

- The Stage Recording Rule: If a camera is set up on the side of a room (e.g., the left side) to record an event where the audience is facing forward toward a stage, the camera is typically facing 'Right' (or towards the center stage) to capture the profile of the event, rather than facing strictly forward.

916.spatial_relations_text:
--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Viewpoint Visibility) ---
- Visibility must always be evaluated from the queried observer's viewpoint, not from the camera's viewpoint. To decide what a seated person or a standing teacher can see, mentally reposition the viewpoint to that person's eye location and facing direction before checking line-of-sight.
- An object that is present in the scene may still be not visible, only partially visible, or not clearly readable from a different viewpoint. Presence in the image does not guarantee visibility from every position within the scene.
- Gaze direction of a person is primarily determined by head and torso orientation, not by proximity on the grid. The nearest object is not necessarily the one being looked at; the object aligned with the person's forward-facing axis is.
- Occlusion is viewpoint-dependent and asymmetric: a smaller foreground object can partially hide a larger background object when they overlap along the viewing ray from the observer. Two objects side by side in the image plane do not occlude each other unless their projected extents actually overlap from the relevant viewpoint.
- For questions about whether text on a sign, shop name, or board is clearly readable, consider the observer's distance, angle of incidence, and whether foreground clutter or structural elements intervene. A sign can be physically present yet unreadable from an oblique seated position.

918.spatial_relations_text:
--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Stability Prediction) ---
- Objects placed flat with their center of gravity within the effective base of support are stable by default.
- Do not predict dramatic or chain-reaction collapses in everyday scenes unless there is clearly insufficient physical support, a steep incline, or a significant external force.
- Perturbations where the agent's mass is less than 1% of the structure's mass (e.g., a bird landing, a raindrop) have no significant effect.
- Perturbations where the agent's mass is greater than 10% of the structure's mass (e.g., a person climbing a light ladder) cause significant displacement or tipping.
- Forces applied above an object's center of gravity tend to cause tipping, while forces applied below tend to cause sliding.
- Precision-balanced structures (e.g., card houses, objects balanced on a point) are extremely vulnerable to minor vibrations, whereas anchored or heavy structures (e.g., lamp posts, trees, wall-mounted TVs) are unaffected by everyday light impacts.
- When determining the 'least stable' object, assess only the geometric ratio of the base of support relative to the height. Fragility does not equal instability.
- On inclines, objects with curved contact surfaces (spheres, sideways cylinders) will roll at minor tilts of 1-2 degrees. Objects with flat bases rely on friction and typically remain stationary up to 5-15 degrees.
- When an object is removed from an arrangement, only the objects that directly depend on it for support will fall. Objects with independent support remain unaffected.
- When a person sits or jumps on heavy furniture (e.g., beds, sofas, benches), the furniture itself remains stable. Only loose, lightweight items on the surface (e.g., pillows, loose papers, unanchored blankets) will bounce or be displaced.
- Humans possess active muscle reflexes to recover balance. They will not fall uncontrollably from minor obstacles or slight forces unless the environment is extremely steep or slippery.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Gravity Effects) ---
- Dense, compact objects fall at similar speeds regardless of their specific shape; their exact impact times rely on specific environmental factors. Only lightweight, thin objects with high surface-area-to-mass ratios fall significantly slower due to air resistance.
- Poured liquids always follow a curved (parabolic) downward trajectory and spread outward immediately upon impacting a surface.
- Rolling strictly requires a curved contact patch with the surface. Objects with flat bases, irregular contact areas, or symmetry-breaking protrusions will not roll, regardless of their overall shape. Round objects roll continuously on slopes, but require an initial kinetic push to roll temporarily on flat surfaces.
- Gravity alone does not break the equilibrium of a stable resting object; movement requires a specific external trigger (e.g., wind, bump, vibration).
- When a supporting surface is tilted slowly and gradually, static friction keeps objects completely stationary until a specific angle threshold is exceeded.
- Rapidly removing a bottom supporting object causes upper objects to drop vertically in place due to inertia. Slowly removing it causes the upper objects to slide or topple as support is gradually lost.
- Buoyancy depends on overall average density. Objects with trapped internal air pockets will float, but physically altering the object to expose or remove those air pockets (e.g., by slicing or crushing) will cause it to sink.
- Collisions involving either a rigid object hitting a soft/elastic surface, or an elastic object hitting a hard surface, will result in a bounce rather than a dead stop.
- Wind-blown lightweight flexible materials (like fabric) do not float perfectly straight; they follow air currents and will wrap around or cling to physical obstacles in their path.
- An obstacle's ability to stop a moving object depends entirely on their relative mass and speed. Light or small obstacles will not stop heavy or fast-moving objects and will simply be knocked aside.
- Objects cannot balance or stand in orientations where their geometry and center of mass fail to form a stable equilibrium.
- If the camera angle obscures the spatial relationship, trajectory path, or landing zone of a falling or moving object, the physical outcome must be evaluated as 'cannot be determined'.
- Always assume the most direct, intuitive, and simple physical outcome for an action (e.g., gently pushed items slide, liquids spread) without over-analyzing for dramatic or extreme results.

- The Euclidean distance between two objects within the scene cannot be accurately deduced merely by comparing their respective distances to the camera; objects sharing a direct structural baseline, such as being placed on the same table or identical shelving unit, possess greater proximity to each other than to isolated objects sharing a similar camera depth.

957.spatial_relations_text:
--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Stability Prediction) ---
- Objects placed flat with their center of gravity within the effective base of support are stable by default.
- Do not predict dramatic or chain-reaction collapses in everyday scenes unless there is clearly insufficient physical support, a steep incline, or a significant external force.
- Perturbations where the agent's mass is less than 1% of the structure's mass (e.g., a bird landing, a raindrop) have no significant effect.
- Perturbations where the agent's mass is greater than 10% of the structure's mass (e.g., a person climbing a light ladder) cause significant displacement or tipping.
- Forces applied above an object's center of gravity tend to cause tipping, while forces applied below tend to cause sliding.
- Precision-balanced structures (e.g., card houses, objects balanced on a point) are extremely vulnerable to minor vibrations, whereas anchored or heavy structures (e.g., lamp posts, trees, wall-mounted TVs) are unaffected by everyday light impacts.
- When determining the 'least stable' object, assess only the geometric ratio of the base of support relative to the height. Fragility does not equal instability.
- On inclines, objects with curved contact surfaces (spheres, sideways cylinders) will roll at minor tilts of 1-2 degrees. Objects with flat bases rely on friction and typically remain stationary up to 5-15 degrees.
- When an object is removed from an arrangement, only the objects that directly depend on it for support will fall. Objects with independent support remain unaffected.
- When a person sits or jumps on heavy furniture (e.g., beds, sofas, benches), the furniture itself remains stable. Only loose, lightweight items on the surface (e.g., pillows, loose papers, unanchored blankets) will bounce or be displaced.
- Humans possess active muscle reflexes to recover balance. They will not fall uncontrollably from minor obstacles or slight forces unless the environment is extremely steep or slippery.

964.spatial_relations_text:
--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Relative Size Comparison) ---
- Pre-calculated 2D sizes cannot be used as physical conclusions. Objects of the same category (e.g., a herd of animals, mass-produced tables) usually share the same physical size; visual differences are due to perspective. Always mentally place them at the same depth when comparing.
- Rotating a rectangular object 90 degrees swaps its visual length and width. Hidden postures (e.g., kneeling, sitting) cannot be used to directly infer true standing height.
- A flexible object wrapped around the outside of another object (e.g., a chain around a gear) inherently has a larger effective physical radius than the enclosed object.
- When evaluating if container A can hold object B, you must subtract the volume of any items already inside container A. Do not rely solely on exterior dimensions.
- Interactions must respect structural mechanics. A tree cannot support the weight of a concrete building; a human body is too short to bridge a wide canal; a tall vertical structure (like a lighthouse) lacks the structural design to act as a horizontal bridge.
- Biological structures have natural growth limits (e.g., inner flower parts will not grow endlessly beyond outer petals). Manufactured items like shoes scale in 3D; a larger size means increased width and volume, not just length.
- Spherical transparent containers globally magnify the objects inside them. Cylindrical transparent containers only stretch objects horizontally.
- Large printed graphics on a surface (e.g., a giant book printed on a tote bag) create a visual illusion of size but do not increase the object's actual physical volume or capacity.
- Due to massive scale disparities, a small foreground object (like a potted plant) can only occlude a tiny fraction (like a single branch) of a massive background object (like a distant tree). It cannot cover half or most of it.
- Using a shorter background set or backdrop reveals more of the actual room behind it, making the entire space appear more expanded and spacious.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Tool Handedness) ---
- The Handle Orientation Rule: If the handle of a tool (such as a spoon, pan, or mop) is pointing to or positioned on the LEFT side of the user's perspective or a reference object, it is ergonomically optimized to be picked up with the LEFT hand. If positioned on the RIGHT, it favors the RIGHT hand.
- The Precision vs. Gross Motor Rule: A person's handedness (dominant hand) is most evident in actions requiring fine motor control and precision (e.g., scooping spice, writing, stirring, manipulating small objects). It is LEAST evident in gross motor actions, passive holding, or bilateral tasks (e.g., plugging a charger, placing a frame, simply holding a phone, or typing).
- The Active Execution Rule: When interacting with an object like a kettle or a tool, the active execution movement (e.g., lifting, pouring, or scooping) is the primary indicator of handedness, rather than passive stabilization actions (e.g., merely holding the handle or placing a lid).

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Proximity Gradients) ---
- Positions described relative to the 'frame' or 'image boundaries', such as the center of the frame or the top of the image, refer exclusively to 2D Cartesian coordinates (X/Y axes) and must not be evaluated using 3D depth relative to the camera (Z axis).
- When two objects differ simultaneously in their horizontal positioning and their depth relative to the camera, their spatial alignment relative to each other is strictly diagonal, regardless of whether their front planes are oriented parallel to the camera.
- When assessing the closest or farthest depth of specific anatomical parts of a subject viewed in a side or diagonal profile, protruding extremities such as the nose, beak, or leading shoulder represent the extreme depth limits and supersede broader anatomical regions like the entire head or body.
- In evaluating the greatest proximity to a specific subject, direct physical contact or visual overlap by foreground environmental elements, such as surrounding grass, leaves, or branches, often takes precedence over biologically attached body parts that are positioned further back in the 3D space.
- The Euclidean distance between two objects within the scene cannot be accurately deduced merely by comparing their respective distances to the camera; objects sharing a direct structural baseline, such as being placed on the same table or identical shelving unit, possess greater proximity to each other than to isolated objects sharing a similar camera depth.

- The Wall-Mount Rule: A portrait, sign, or object hung on a wall that faces directly into the room is geometrically considered to be facing 'Away' from the wall itself.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Relative Size Comparison) ---
- Pre-calculated 2D sizes cannot be used as physical conclusions. Objects of the same category (e.g., a herd of animals, mass-produced tables) usually share the same physical size; visual differences are due to perspective. Always mentally place them at the same depth when comparing.
- Rotating a rectangular object 90 degrees swaps its visual length and width. Hidden postures (e.g., kneeling, sitting) cannot be used to directly infer true standing height.
- A flexible object wrapped around the outside of another object (e.g., a chain around a gear) inherently has a larger effective physical radius than the enclosed object.
- When evaluating if container A can hold object B, you must subtract the volume of any items already inside container A. Do not rely solely on exterior dimensions.
- Interactions must respect structural mechanics. A tree cannot support the weight of a concrete building; a human body is too short to bridge a wide canal; a tall vertical structure (like a lighthouse) lacks the structural design to act as a horizontal bridge.
- Biological structures have natural growth limits (e.g., inner flower parts will not grow endlessly beyond outer petals). Manufactured items like shoes scale in 3D; a larger size means increased width and volume, not just length.
- Spherical transparent containers globally magnify the objects inside them. Cylindrical transparent containers only stretch objects horizontally.
- Large printed graphics on a surface (e.g., a giant book printed on a tote bag) create a visual illusion of size but do not increase the object's actual physical volume or capacity.
- Due to massive scale disparities, a small foreground object (like a potted plant) can only occlude a tiny fraction (like a single branch) of a massive background object (like a distant tree). It cannot cover half or most of it.
- Using a shorter background set or backdrop reveals more of the actual room behind it, making the entire space appear more expanded and spacious.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Gravity Effects) ---
- Dense, compact objects fall at similar speeds regardless of their specific shape; their exact impact times rely on specific environmental factors. Only lightweight, thin objects with high surface-area-to-mass ratios fall significantly slower due to air resistance.
- Poured liquids always follow a curved (parabolic) downward trajectory and spread outward immediately upon impacting a surface.
- Rolling strictly requires a curved contact patch with the surface. Objects with flat bases, irregular contact areas, or symmetry-breaking protrusions will not roll, regardless of their overall shape. Round objects roll continuously on slopes, but require an initial kinetic push to roll temporarily on flat surfaces.
- Gravity alone does not break the equilibrium of a stable resting object; movement requires a specific external trigger (e.g., wind, bump, vibration).
- When a supporting surface is tilted slowly and gradually, static friction keeps objects completely stationary until a specific angle threshold is exceeded.
- Rapidly removing a bottom supporting object causes upper objects to drop vertically in place due to inertia. Slowly removing it causes the upper objects to slide or topple as support is gradually lost.
- Buoyancy depends on overall average density. Objects with trapped internal air pockets will float, but physically altering the object to expose or remove those air pockets (e.g., by slicing or crushing) will cause it to sink.
- Collisions involving either a rigid object hitting a soft/elastic surface, or an elastic object hitting a hard surface, will result in a bounce rather than a dead stop.
- Wind-blown lightweight flexible materials (like fabric) do not float perfectly straight; they follow air currents and will wrap around or cling to physical obstacles in their path.
- An obstacle's ability to stop a moving object depends entirely on their relative mass and speed. Light or small obstacles will not stop heavy or fast-moving objects and will simply be knocked aside.
- Objects cannot balance or stand in orientations where their geometry and center of mass fail to form a stable equilibrium.
- If the camera angle obscures the spatial relationship, trajectory path, or landing zone of a falling or moving object, the physical outcome must be evaluated as 'cannot be determined'.
- Always assume the most direct, intuitive, and simple physical outcome for an action (e.g., gently pushed items slide, liquids spread) without over-analyzing for dramatic or extreme results.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Tool Handedness) ---
- The Handle Orientation Rule: If the handle of a tool (such as a spoon, pan, or mop) is pointing to or positioned on the LEFT side of the user's perspective or a reference object, it is ergonomically optimized to be picked up with the LEFT hand. If positioned on the RIGHT, it favors the RIGHT hand.
- The Precision vs. Gross Motor Rule: A person's handedness (dominant hand) is most evident in actions requiring fine motor control and precision (e.g., scooping spice, writing, stirring, manipulating small objects). It is LEAST evident in gross motor actions, passive holding, or bilateral tasks (e.g., plugging a charger, placing a frame, simply holding a phone, or typing).
- The Active Execution Rule: When interacting with an object like a kettle or a tool, the active execution movement (e.g., lifting, pouring, or scooping) is the primary indicator of handedness, rather than passive stabilization actions (e.g., merely holding the handle or placing a lid).

990.spatial_relations_text:
--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Viewpoint Visibility) ---
- Visibility must always be evaluated from the queried observer's viewpoint, not from the camera's viewpoint. To decide what a seated person or a standing teacher can see, mentally reposition the viewpoint to that person's eye location and facing direction before checking line-of-sight.
- An object that is present in the scene may still be not visible, only partially visible, or not clearly readable from a different viewpoint. Presence in the image does not guarantee visibility from every position within the scene.
- Gaze direction of a person is primarily determined by head and torso orientation, not by proximity on the grid. The nearest object is not necessarily the one being looked at; the object aligned with the person's forward-facing axis is.
- Occlusion is viewpoint-dependent and asymmetric: a smaller foreground object can partially hide a larger background object when they overlap along the viewing ray from the observer. Two objects side by side in the image plane do not occlude each other unless their projected extents actually overlap from the relevant viewpoint.
- For questions about whether text on a sign, shop name, or board is clearly readable, consider the observer's distance, angle of incidence, and whether foreground clutter or structural elements intervene. A sign can be physically present yet unreadable from an oblique seated position.
- Furniture such as benches, sofas, desks, and stadium seating imposes a natural facing direction. A person sitting on a bench typically faces the open or focal side. Use this prior to infer what they can or cannot see.
- When counting fully visible people, an individual is fully visible only if no part of their body is materially clipped by the image boundary or occluded by another object. Partially cropped or overlapping figures should not be counted as fully visible.
- Objects placed behind other objects of the same semantic category (e.g., a large vase behind a smaller vase) can still be partially hidden. Occlusion depends on spatial overlap in projection, not on category membership.

- Interactions must respect structural mechanics. A tree cannot support the weight of a concrete building; a human body is too short to bridge a wide canal; a tall vertical structure (like a lighthouse) lacks the structural design to act as a horizontal bridge.
- Biological structures have natural growth limits (e.g., inner flower parts will not grow endlessly beyond outer petals). Manufactured items like shoes scale in 3D; a larger size means increased width and volume, not just length.
- Spherical transparent containers globally magnify the objects inside them. Cylindrical transparent containers only stretch objects horizontally.
- Large printed graphics on a surface (e.g., a giant book printed on a tote bag) create a visual illusion of size but do not increase the object's actual physical volume or capacity.
- Due to massive scale disparities, a small foreground object (like a potted plant) can only occlude a tiny fraction (like a single branch) of a massive background object (like a distant tree). It cannot cover half or most of it.
- Using a shorter background set or backdrop reveals more of the actual room behind it, making the entire space appear more expanded and spacious.

1011.spatial_relations_text:
Bypassed via Pure VLM.
--- Domain Rules (Accessibility Constraints) ---
- Systematically count every distinct physical item blocking an access point. For grouped items or stacks, count each visually distinguishable unit separately. Do not stop at only the most salient objects.
- An access zone includes the entire physical footprint needed to approach, operate, and pass through an opening (e.g., a door's swing arc). Any object intersecting this extended zone is a blocker.
- A 'direct path' requires continuous, same-surface traversal. Solid physical dividers (e.g., medians, barriers, grade separations) block direct paths.
- To identify a specific obstructing object, trace a direct approach line to the target. The first object intersecting this line (including items protruding from above or below) is the primary obstructor.
- Human vertical reach is limited (~2.0-2.3m). Objects or surfaces above ground-level reach (e.g., high shelves, ceilings) are inaccessible without visual evidence of specialized equipment.
- Select the most specific, visually verifiable reason for inaccessibility (e.g., 'no visible stairs'). Avoid defaulting to compound answers like 'all of the above' unless every factor is independently confirmed.
- 'Available' strictly means physically present, unoccupied, and usable. Occupied slots or seats count as zero availability.
- Evaluate obstructions for different directions or zones independently. Multiple areas can be obstructed simultaneously, so always verify compound options (e.g., 'both X and Y').
- In structured environments (e.g., aisles, marked lots), assume a path is accessible if no physical obstacles are visibly present. The absence of visible blockage means the path is clear.
- Limit a blocked zone strictly to the physical footprint of the obstructing object. Do not extrapolate a blockage to adjacent, unoccluded access points.

1012.spatial_relations_text:
Bypassed via Pure VLM.
--- Domain Rules (Scale Consistency) ---
- Capacity depends on 3D internal volume (depth, width, opening), not just 2D footprint or length. Without 3D data, differently shaped containers are non-measurable. Sugarcane yield depends on thickness, not just length.
- Appropriateness requires matching functional capacity to the space. A single small AC or a small chandelier is insufficient for a large room/hall, regardless of whether it physically 'fits'.
- When evaluating 'fit inside', consider all valid placements: internal shelves, top ledges, or vertical stacking (e.g., standardized laundry appliances are stackable).
- A background or backdrop makes the foreground object appear BIGGER and more prominent through framing contrast, not smaller.
- Objects appearing smaller in the distance (e.g., top stairs, distant fruits) is purely perspective shrinkage. Physically, they are the same size as the closer ones.
- Mass-produced industrial items (e.g., thread spools, bowls, water dispensers) are physically identical in size. Apparent size differences in a single image are always due to perspective or angle.
- Biological reality overrides naive visual impressions: a baby is small enough to ride a dog; a bird's neck can extend significantly; a tree's leaf canopy visually inflates its mass.
- Seating capacity relies on table perimeter (approx. 60cm per person). Two long tables can easily seat 10+ people. Do not default to small fixed numbers without visual proof.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Gravity Effects) ---
- Dense, compact objects fall at similar speeds regardless of their specific shape; their exact impact times rely on specific environmental factors. Only lightweight, thin objects with high surface-area-to-mass ratios fall significantly slower due to air resistance.
- Poured liquids always follow a curved (parabolic) downward trajectory and spread outward immediately upon impacting a surface.
- Rolling strictly requires a curved contact patch with the surface. Objects with flat bases, irregular contact areas, or symmetry-breaking protrusions will not roll, regardless of their overall shape. Round objects roll continuously on slopes, but require an initial kinetic push to roll temporarily on flat surfaces.
- Gravity alone does not break the equilibrium of a stable resting object; movement requires a specific external trigger (e.g., wind, bump, vibration).
- When a supporting surface is tilted slowly and gradually, static friction keeps objects completely stationary until a specific angle threshold is exceeded.
- Rapidly removing a bottom supporting object causes upper objects to drop vertically in place due to inertia. Slowly removing it causes the upper objects to slide or topple as support is gradually lost.
- Buoyancy depends on overall average density. Objects with trapped internal air pockets will float, but physically altering the object to expose or remove those air pockets (e.g., by slicing or crushing) will cause it to sink.
- Collisions involving either a rigid object hitting a soft/elastic surface, or an elastic object hitting a hard surface, will result in a bounce rather than a dead stop.
- Wind-blown lightweight flexible materials (like fabric) do not float perfectly straight; they follow air currents and will wrap around or cling to physical obstacles in their path.
- An obstacle's ability to stop a moving object depends entirely on their relative mass and speed. Light or small obstacles will not stop heavy or fast-moving objects and will simply be knocked aside.
- Objects cannot balance or stand in orientations where their geometry and center of mass fail to form a stable equilibrium.
- If the camera angle obscures the spatial relationship, trajectory path, or landing zone of a falling or moving object, the physical outcome must be evaluated as 'cannot be determined'.
- Always assume the most direct, intuitive, and simple physical outcome for an action (e.g., gently pushed items slide, liquids spread) without over-analyzing for dramatic or extreme results.

--- Domain Rules (Stability Prediction) ---
- Objects placed flat with their center of gravity within the effective base of support are stable by default.
- Do not predict dramatic or chain-reaction collapses in everyday scenes unless there is clearly insufficient physical support, a steep incline, or a significant external force.
- Perturbations where the agent's mass is less than 1% of the structure's mass (e.g., a bird landing, a raindrop) have no significant effect.
- Perturbations where the agent's mass is greater than 10% of the structure's mass (e.g., a person climbing a light ladder) cause significant displacement or tipping.
- Forces applied above an object's center of gravity tend to cause tipping, while forces applied below tend to cause sliding.
- Precision-balanced structures (e.g., card houses, objects balanced on a point) are extremely vulnerable to minor vibrations, whereas anchored or heavy structures (e.g., lamp posts, trees, wall-mounted TVs) are unaffected by everyday light impacts.
- When determining the 'least stable' object, assess only the geometric ratio of the base of support relative to the height. Fragility does not equal instability.
- On inclines, objects with curved contact surfaces (spheres, sideways cylinders) will roll at minor tilts of 1-2 degrees. Objects with flat bases rely on friction and typically remain stationary up to 5-15 degrees.
- When an object is removed from an arrangement, only the objects that directly depend on it for support will fall. Objects with independent support remain unaffected.
- When a person sits or jumps on heavy furniture (e.g., beds, sofas, benches), the furniture itself remains stable. Only loose, lightweight items on the surface (e.g., pillows, loose papers, unanchored blankets) will bounce or be displaced.
- Humans possess active muscle reflexes to recover balance. They will not fall uncontrollably from minor obstacles or slight forces unless the environment is extremely steep or slippery.

1018.spatial_relations_text:

--- Domain Rules (Scale Consistency) ---
- Capacity depends on 3D internal volume (depth, width, opening), not just 2D footprint or length. Without 3D data, differently shaped containers are non-measurable. Sugarcane yield depends on thickness, not just length.
- Appropriateness requires matching functional capacity to the space. A single small AC or a small chandelier is insufficient for a large room/hall, regardless of whether it physically 'fits'.
- When evaluating 'fit inside', consider all valid placements: internal shelves, top ledges, or vertical stacking (e.g., standardized laundry appliances are stackable).
- A background or backdrop makes the foreground object appear BIGGER and more prominent through framing contrast, not smaller.
- Objects appearing smaller in the distance (e.g., top stairs, distant fruits) is purely perspective shrinkage. Physically, they are the same size as the closer ones.
- Mass-produced industrial items (e.g., thread spools, bowls, water dispensers) are physically identical in size. Apparent size differences in a single image are always due to perspective or angle.
- Biological reality overrides naive visual impressions: a baby is small enough to ride a dog; a bird's neck can extend significantly; a tree's leaf canopy visually inflates its mass.
- Seating capacity relies on table perimeter (approx. 60cm per person). Two long tables can easily seat 10+ people. Do not default to small fixed numbers without visual proof.

1035.spatial_relations_text:
Bypassed via Pure VLM.
--- Domain Rules (Accessibility Constraints) ---
- Systematically count every distinct physical item blocking an access point. For grouped items or stacks, count each visually distinguishable unit separately. Do not stop at only the most salient objects.
- An access zone includes the entire physical footprint needed to approach, operate, and pass through an opening (e.g., a door's swing arc). Any object intersecting this extended zone is a blocker.
- A 'direct path' requires continuous, same-surface traversal. Solid physical dividers (e.g., medians, barriers, grade separations) block direct paths.
- To identify a specific obstructing object, trace a direct approach line to the target. The first object intersecting this line (including items protruding from above or below) is the primary obstructor.
- Human vertical reach is limited (~2.0-2.3m). Objects or surfaces above ground-level reach (e.g., high shelves, ceilings) are inaccessible without visual evidence of specialized equipment.
- Select the most specific, visually verifiable reason for inaccessibility (e.g., 'no visible stairs'). Avoid defaulting to compound answers like 'all of the above' unless every factor is independently confirmed.
- 'Available' strictly means physically present, unoccupied, and usable. Occupied slots or seats count as zero availability.
- Evaluate obstructions for different directions or zones independently. Multiple areas can be obstructed simultaneously, so always verify compound options (e.g., 'both X and Y').
- In structured environments (e.g., aisles, marked lots), assume a path is accessible if no physical obstacles are visibly present. The absence of visible blockage means the path is clear.
- Limit a blocked zone strictly to the physical footprint of the obstructing object. Do not extrapolate a blockage to adjacent, unoccluded access points.

1040.spatial_relations_text:
--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Viewpoint Visibility) ---
- Visibility must always be evaluated from the queried observer's viewpoint, not from the camera's viewpoint. To decide what a seated person or a standing teacher can see, mentally reposition the viewpoint to that person's eye location and facing direction before checking line-of-sight.
- An object that is present in the scene may still be not visible, only partially visible, or not clearly readable from a different viewpoint. Presence in the image does not guarantee visibility from every position within the scene.
- Gaze direction of a person is primarily determined by head and torso orientation, not by proximity on the grid. The nearest object is not necessarily the one being looked at; the object aligned with the person's forward-facing axis is.
- Occlusion is viewpoint-dependent and asymmetric: a smaller foreground object can partially hide a larger background object when they overlap along the viewing ray from the observer. Two objects side by side in the image plane do not occlude each other unless their projected extents actually overlap from the relevant viewpoint.
- For questions about whether text on a sign, shop name, or board is clearly readable, consider the observer's distance, angle of incidence, and whether foreground clutter or structural elements intervene. A sign can be physically present yet unreadable from an oblique seated position.
- Furniture such as benches, sofas, desks, and stadium seating imposes a natural facing direction. A person sitting on a bench typically faces the open or focal side. Use this prior to infer what they can or cannot see.
- When counting fully visible people, an individual is fully visible only if no part of their body is materially clipped by the image boundary or occluded by another object. Partially cropped or overlapping figures should not be counted as fully visible.
- Objects placed behind other objects of the same semantic category (e.g., a large vase behind a smaller vase) can still be partially hidden. Occlusion depends on spatial overlap in projection, not on category membership.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Relative Size Comparison) ---
- Pre-calculated 2D sizes cannot be used as physical conclusions. Objects of the same category (e.g., a herd of animals, mass-produced tables) usually share the same physical size; visual differences are due to perspective. Always mentally place them at the same depth when comparing.
- Rotating a rectangular object 90 degrees swaps its visual length and width. Hidden postures (e.g., kneeling, sitting) cannot be used to directly infer true standing height.
- A flexible object wrapped around the outside of another object (e.g., a chain around a gear) inherently has a larger effective physical radius than the enclosed object.
- When evaluating if container A can hold object B, you must subtract the volume of any items already inside container A. Do not rely solely on exterior dimensions.
- Interactions must respect structural mechanics. A tree cannot support the weight of a concrete building; a human body is too short to bridge a wide canal; a tall vertical structure (like a lighthouse) lacks the structural design to act as a horizontal bridge.
- Biological structures have natural growth limits (e.g., inner flower parts will not grow endlessly beyond outer petals). Manufactured items like shoes scale in 3D; a larger size means increased width and volume, not just length.
- Spherical transparent containers globally magnify the objects inside them. Cylindrical transparent containers only stretch objects horizontally.
- Large printed graphics on a surface (e.g., a giant book printed on a tote bag) create a visual illusion of size but do not increase the object's actual physical volume or capacity.
- Due to massive scale disparities, a small foreground object (like a potted plant) can only occlude a tiny fraction (like a single branch) of a massive background object (like a distant tree). It cannot cover half or most of it.
- Using a shorter background set or backdrop reveals more of the actual room behind it, making the entire space appear more expanded and spacious.

--- Domain Rules (Gravity Effects) ---
- Dense, compact objects fall at similar speeds regardless of their specific shape; their exact impact times rely on specific environmental factors. Only lightweight, thin objects with high surface-area-to-mass ratios fall significantly slower due to air resistance.
- Poured liquids always follow a curved (parabolic) downward trajectory and spread outward immediately upon impacting a surface.
- Rolling strictly requires a curved contact patch with the surface. Objects with flat bases, irregular contact areas, or symmetry-breaking protrusions will not roll, regardless of their overall shape. Round objects roll continuously on slopes, but require an initial kinetic push to roll temporarily on flat surfaces.
- Gravity alone does not break the equilibrium of a stable resting object; movement requires a specific external trigger (e.g., wind, bump, vibration).
- When a supporting surface is tilted slowly and gradually, static friction keeps objects completely stationary until a specific angle threshold is exceeded.
- Rapidly removing a bottom supporting object causes upper objects to drop vertically in place due to inertia. Slowly removing it causes the upper objects to slide or topple as support is gradually lost.
- Buoyancy depends on overall average density. Objects with trapped internal air pockets will float, but physically altering the object to expose or remove those air pockets (e.g., by slicing or crushing) will cause it to sink.
- Collisions involving either a rigid object hitting a soft/elastic surface, or an elastic object hitting a hard surface, will result in a bounce rather than a dead stop.
- Wind-blown lightweight flexible materials (like fabric) do not float perfectly straight; they follow air currents and will wrap around or cling to physical obstacles in their path.
- An obstacle's ability to stop a moving object depends entirely on their relative mass and speed. Light or small obstacles will not stop heavy or fast-moving objects and will simply be knocked aside.
- Objects cannot balance or stand in orientations where their geometry and center of mass fail to form a stable equilibrium.
- If the camera angle obscures the spatial relationship, trajectory path, or landing zone of a falling or moving object, the physical outcome must be evaluated as 'cannot be determined'.
- Always assume the most direct, intuitive, and simple physical outcome for an action (e.g., gently pushed items slide, liquids spread) without over-analyzing for dramatic or extreme results.

1054.spatial_relations_text:
Bypassed via Pure VLM.
--- Domain Rules (Accessibility Constraints) ---
- Systematically count every distinct physical item blocking an access point. For grouped items or stacks, count each visually distinguishable unit separately. Do not stop at only the most salient objects.
- An access zone includes the entire physical footprint needed to approach, operate, and pass through an opening (e.g., a door's swing arc). Any object intersecting this extended zone is a blocker.
- A 'direct path' requires continuous, same-surface traversal. Solid physical dividers (e.g., medians, barriers, grade separations) block direct paths.
- To identify a specific obstructing object, trace a direct approach line to the target. The first object intersecting this line (including items protruding from above or below) is the primary obstructor.
- Human vertical reach is limited (~2.0-2.3m). Objects or surfaces above ground-level reach (e.g., high shelves, ceilings) are inaccessible without visual evidence of specialized equipment.
- Select the most specific, visually verifiable reason for inaccessibility (e.g., 'no visible stairs'). Avoid defaulting to compound answers like 'all of the above' unless every factor is independently confirmed.
- 'Available' strictly means physically present, unoccupied, and usable. Occupied slots or seats count as zero availability.
- Evaluate obstructions for different directions or zones independently. Multiple areas can be obstructed simultaneously, so always verify compound options (e.g., 'both X and Y').
- In structured environments (e.g., aisles, marked lots), assume a path is accessible if no physical obstacles are visibly present. The absence of visible blockage means the path is clear.
- Limit a blocked zone strictly to the physical footprint of the obstructing object. Do not extrapolate a blockage to adjacent, unoccluded access points.

1055.spatial_relations_text:
Bypassed via Pure VLM.
--- Domain Rules (Scale Consistency) ---
- Capacity depends on 3D internal volume (depth, width, opening), not just 2D footprint or length. Without 3D data, differently shaped containers are non-measurable. Sugarcane yield depends on thickness, not just length.
- Appropriateness requires matching functional capacity to the space. A single small AC or a small chandelier is insufficient for a large room/hall, regardless of whether it physically 'fits'.
- When evaluating 'fit inside', consider all valid placements: internal shelves, top ledges, or vertical stacking (e.g., standardized laundry appliances are stackable).
- A background or backdrop makes the foreground object appear BIGGER and more prominent through framing contrast, not smaller.
- Objects appearing smaller in the distance (e.g., top stairs, distant fruits) is purely perspective shrinkage. Physically, they are the same size as the closer ones.
- Mass-produced industrial items (e.g., thread spools, bowls, water dispensers) are physically identical in size. Apparent size differences in a single image are always due to perspective or angle.
- Biological reality overrides naive visual impressions: a baby is small enough to ride a dog; a bird's neck can extend significantly; a tree's leaf canopy visually inflates its mass.
- Seating capacity relies on table perimeter (approx. 60cm per person). Two long tables can easily seat 10+ people. Do not default to small fixed numbers without visual proof.

1062.spatial_relations_text:
--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Stability Prediction) ---
- Objects placed flat with their center of gravity within the effective base of support are stable by default.
- Do not predict dramatic or chain-reaction collapses in everyday scenes unless there is clearly insufficient physical support, a steep incline, or a significant external force.
- Perturbations where the agent's mass is less than 1% of the structure's mass (e.g., a bird landing, a raindrop) have no significant effect.
- Perturbations where the agent's mass is greater than 10% of the structure's mass (e.g., a person climbing a light ladder) cause significant displacement or tipping.
- Forces applied above an object's center of gravity tend to cause tipping, while forces applied below tend to cause sliding.
- Precision-balanced structures (e.g., card houses, objects balanced on a point) are extremely vulnerable to minor vibrations, whereas anchored or heavy structures (e.g., lamp posts, trees, wall-mounted TVs) are unaffected by everyday light impacts.
- When determining the 'least stable' object, assess only the geometric ratio of the base of support relative to the height. Fragility does not equal instability.
- On inclines, objects with curved contact surfaces (spheres, sideways cylinders) will roll at minor tilts of 1-2 degrees. Objects with flat bases rely on friction and typically remain stationary up to 5-15 degrees.
- When an object is removed from an arrangement, only the objects that directly depend on it for support will fall. Objects with independent support remain unaffected.
- When a person sits or jumps on heavy furniture (e.g., beds, sofas, benches), the furniture itself remains stable. Only loose, lightweight items on the surface (e.g., pillows, loose papers, unanchored blankets) will bounce or be displaced.
- Humans possess active muscle reflexes to recover balance. They will not fall uncontrollably from minor obstacles or slight forces unless the environment is extremely steep or slippery.

--- Domain Rules (Tool Handedness) ---
- The Handle Orientation Rule: If the handle of a tool (such as a spoon, pan, or mop) is pointing to or positioned on the LEFT side of the user's perspective or a reference object, it is ergonomically optimized to be picked up with the LEFT hand. If positioned on the RIGHT, it favors the RIGHT hand.
- The Precision vs. Gross Motor Rule: A person's handedness (dominant hand) is most evident in actions requiring fine motor control and precision (e.g., scooping spice, writing, stirring, manipulating small objects). It is LEAST evident in gross motor actions, passive holding, or bilateral tasks (e.g., plugging a charger, placing a frame, simply holding a phone, or typing).
- The Active Execution Rule: When interacting with an object like a kettle or a tool, the active execution movement (e.g., lifting, pouring, or scooping) is the primary indicator of handedness, rather than passive stabilization actions (e.g., merely holding the handle or placing a lid).

1078.spatial_relations_text:
Bypassed via Pure VLM.
--- Domain Rules (Accessibility Constraints) ---
- Systematically count every distinct physical item blocking an access point. For grouped items or stacks, count each visually distinguishable unit separately. Do not stop at only the most salient objects.
- An access zone includes the entire physical footprint needed to approach, operate, and pass through an opening (e.g., a door's swing arc). Any object intersecting this extended zone is a blocker.
- A 'direct path' requires continuous, same-surface traversal. Solid physical dividers (e.g., medians, barriers, grade separations) block direct paths.
- To identify a specific obstructing object, trace a direct approach line to the target. The first object intersecting this line (including items protruding from above or below) is the primary obstructor.
- Human vertical reach is limited (~2.0-2.3m). Objects or surfaces above ground-level reach (e.g., high shelves, ceilings) are inaccessible without visual evidence of specialized equipment.
- Select the most specific, visually verifiable reason for inaccessibility (e.g., 'no visible stairs'). Avoid defaulting to compound answers like 'all of the above' unless every factor is independently confirmed.
- 'Available' strictly means physically present, unoccupied, and usable. Occupied slots or seats count as zero availability.
- Evaluate obstructions for different directions or zones independently. Multiple areas can be obstructed simultaneously, so always verify compound options (e.g., 'both X and Y').
- In structured environments (e.g., aisles, marked lots), assume a path is accessible if no physical obstacles are visibly present. The absence of visible blockage means the path is clear.
- Limit a blocked zone strictly to the physical footprint of the obstructing object. Do not extrapolate a blockage to adjacent, unoccluded access points.

=== Size & Height Deductions ===
- Not enough valid physical dimensions extracted to compare sizes.

=== Horizontal Level Alignment ===

=== Occlusion & Depth Deductions ===
- No significant occlusion detected among the extracted objects.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Relative Size Comparison) ---
- Pre-calculated 2D sizes cannot be used as physical conclusions. Objects of the same category (e.g., a herd of animals, mass-produced tables) usually share the same physical size; visual differences are due to perspective. Always mentally place them at the same depth when comparing.
- Rotating a rectangular object 90 degrees swaps its visual length and width. Hidden postures (e.g., kneeling, sitting) cannot be used to directly infer true standing height.
- A flexible object wrapped around the outside of another object (e.g., a chain around a gear) inherently has a larger effective physical radius than the enclosed object.
- When evaluating if container A can hold object B, you must subtract the volume of any items already inside container A. Do not rely solely on exterior dimensions.
- Interactions must respect structural mechanics. A tree cannot support the weight of a concrete building; a human body is too short to bridge a wide canal; a tall vertical structure (like a lighthouse) lacks the structural design to act as a horizontal bridge.
- Biological structures have natural growth limits (e.g., inner flower parts will not grow endlessly beyond outer petals). Manufactured items like shoes scale in 3D; a larger size means increased width and volume, not just length.
- Spherical transparent containers globally magnify the objects inside them. Cylindrical transparent containers only stretch objects horizontally.
- Large printed graphics on a surface (e.g., a giant book printed on a tote bag) create a visual illusion of size but do not increase the object's actual physical volume or capacity.
- Due to massive scale disparities, a small foreground object (like a potted plant) can only occlude a tiny fraction (like a single branch) of a massive background object (like a distant tree). It cannot cover half or most of it.
- Using a shorter background set or backdrop reveals more of the actual room behind it, making the entire space appear more expanded and spacious.

1082.spatial_relations_text:
=== Single Object Geometric States ===
- [lemon wedge]: Presents a strict 'side_profile' view pointing 'right'. This indicates it is positioned laterally (side-ways).

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Gravity Effects) ---
- Dense, compact objects fall at similar speeds regardless of their specific shape; their exact impact times rely on specific environmental factors. Only lightweight, thin objects with high surface-area-to-mass ratios fall significantly slower due to air resistance.
- Poured liquids always follow a curved (parabolic) downward trajectory and spread outward immediately upon impacting a surface.
- Rolling strictly requires a curved contact patch with the surface. Objects with flat bases, irregular contact areas, or symmetry-breaking protrusions will not roll, regardless of their overall shape. Round objects roll continuously on slopes, but require an initial kinetic push to roll temporarily on flat surfaces.
- Gravity alone does not break the equilibrium of a stable resting object; movement requires a specific external trigger (e.g., wind, bump, vibration).
- When a supporting surface is tilted slowly and gradually, static friction keeps objects completely stationary until a specific angle threshold is exceeded.
- Rapidly removing a bottom supporting object causes upper objects to drop vertically in place due to inertia. Slowly removing it causes the upper objects to slide or topple as support is gradually lost.
- Buoyancy depends on overall average density. Objects with trapped internal air pockets will float, but physically altering the object to expose or remove those air pockets (e.g., by slicing or crushing) will cause it to sink.
- Collisions involving either a rigid object hitting a soft/elastic surface, or an elastic object hitting a hard surface, will result in a bounce rather than a dead stop.
- Wind-blown lightweight flexible materials (like fabric) do not float perfectly straight; they follow air currents and will wrap around or cling to physical obstacles in their path.
- An obstacle's ability to stop a moving object depends entirely on their relative mass and speed. Light or small obstacles will not stop heavy or fast-moving objects and will simply be knocked aside.
- Objects cannot balance or stand in orientations where their geometry and center of mass fail to form a stable equilibrium.
- If the camera angle obscures the spatial relationship, trajectory path, or landing zone of a falling or moving object, the physical outcome must be evaluated as 'cannot be determined'.
- Always assume the most direct, intuitive, and simple physical outcome for an action (e.g., gently pushed items slide, liquids spread) without over-analyzing for dramatic or extreme results.

--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Proximity Gradients) ---
- Positions described relative to the 'frame' or 'image boundaries', such as the center of the frame or the top of the image, refer exclusively to 2D Cartesian coordinates (X/Y axes) and must not be evaluated using 3D depth relative to the camera (Z axis).
- When two objects differ simultaneously in their horizontal positioning and their depth relative to the camera, their spatial alignment relative to each other is strictly diagonal, regardless of whether their front planes are oriented parallel to the camera.
- When assessing the closest or farthest depth of specific anatomical parts of a subject viewed in a side or diagonal profile, protruding extremities such as the nose, beak, or leading shoulder represent the extreme depth limits and supersede broader anatomical regions like the entire head or body.
- In evaluating the greatest proximity to a specific subject, direct physical contact or visual overlap by foreground environmental elements, such as surrounding grass, leaves, or branches, often takes precedence over biologically attached body parts that are positioned further back in the 3D space.
- The Euclidean distance between two objects within the scene cannot be accurately deduced merely by comparing their respective distances to the camera; objects sharing a direct structural baseline, such as being placed on the same table or identical shelving unit, possess greater proximity to each other than to isolated objects sharing a similar camera depth.

1095.spatial_relations_text:
--- EXTERNAL DOMAIN KNOWLEDGE ---
--- Domain Rules (Viewpoint Visibility) ---
- Visibility must always be evaluated from the queried observer's viewpoint, not from the camera's viewpoint. To decide what a seated person or a standing teacher can see, mentally reposition the viewpoint to that person's eye location and facing direction before checking line-of-sight.
- An object that is present in the scene may still be not visible, only partially visible, or not clearly readable from a different viewpoint. Presence in the image does not guarantee visibility from every position within the scene.
- Gaze direction of a person is primarily determined by head and torso orientation, not by proximity on the grid. The nearest object is not necessarily the one being looked at; the object aligned with the person's forward-facing axis is.
- Occlusion is viewpoint-dependent and asymmetric: a smaller foreground object can partially hide a larger background object when they overlap along the viewing ray from the observer. Two objects side by side in the image plane do not occlude each other unless their projected extents actually overlap from the relevant viewpoint.
- For questions about whether text on a sign, shop name, or board is clearly readable, consider the observer's distance, angle of incidence, and whether foreground clutter or structural elements intervene. A sign can be physically present yet unreadable from an oblique seated position.
- Furniture such as benches, sofas, desks, and stadium seating imposes a natural facing direction. A person sitting on a bench typically faces the open or focal side. Use this prior to infer what they can or cannot see.
- When counting fully visible people, an individual is fully visible only if no part of their body is materially clipped by the image boundary or occluded by another object. Partially cropped or overlapping figures should not be counted as fully visible.
- Objects placed behind other objects of the same semantic category (e.g., a large vase behind a smaller vase) can still be partially hidden. Occlusion depends on spatial overlap in projection, not on category membership.
